link|Q66085127}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushitama Shrine |Image = [[File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Main hall.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}{{jalink|Sakuragawa City|Q990350}}Kamobe 694 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Nushitama-no-Kami}} |Shintai = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 8th year of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Chief priest = |Main sanctuary = {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other names = Kamo Shrine |Pilgrimage affiliation = |Annual festival = November 14–15 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushitama Shrine''' (かもおおかみみこかみぬしたまじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine enshrining Nushitama-no-Kami, located along the Sakuragawa River in Kamobe, Sakuragawa City, Ibaraki Prefecture. It is identified with the Shikinaisha (minor shrine) “Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushi Shrine” of Niihari District in Hitachi Province. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was classified as a gōsha (village shrine). {{Double image aside|right|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Hiejin.JPG|160|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kashima.JPG|160|{{center|Auxiliary shrine: Hie Shrine}}|{{center|Auxiliary shrine: Kashima Shrine}}}} {{Triple image aside|right|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kasuga.JPG|120|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kotohira.JPG|120|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Hachiman.JPG|120|{{center|Sub-shrine: Kasuga Shrine}}|{{center|Sub-shrine: Kotohira Shrine}}|{{center|Sub-shrine: Hachiman Shrine}}}} === Enshrined Deities === * {{jalink|賀茂別雷命|Q11634943|別雷神}} (Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami) * {{jalink|大田田根子|Q55536798|大田々根子神}} (Ōtatane-ko-no-Kami) * {{jalink|主玉神}} (Nushitama-no-Kami) In {{jalink|府県郷社明治神社誌料|Q11486103|Meiji Shrine Records}}, Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto and Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami are listed as the two enshrined deities. In the *Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album*, the principal enshrined deity is Kamo Ōkami no Miko no Kami Nushitama-no-Kami, with Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto and Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto as associated deities. === Shrine Precinct Shrines === ==== Subsidiary Shrines ==== * Hie Shrine – (principal enshrined deity) Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto * Kashima Shrine – (principal enshrined deity) Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto ==== Minor Shrines ==== * Ōsugi Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Kotohira Shrine (Konpira Shrine) * Tenmangū (Tenman Ōkami) * Hakusan Shrine * Kasuga Shrine * Inari Shrine In {{jalink|府県郷社明治神社誌料|Q11486103|Meiji Shrine Records}}, eight shrines are listed: Hie Shrine, Kashima Shrine, Inari Shrine, Kasuga Shrine, Tenmangū, Hakusan Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Kotohira Shrine. === Festivals === * Omezame Festival (January 1) * National Foundation Festival (February 11) * Gion Festival (July 24–26) * Grand Festival (November 14–15) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Winter Solstice Festival (around December 22) * Oyasumi Festival and First Door Opening (December 31) Notice board at the shrine precincts: Annual Events. Until around 1985 (Shōwa 60), the First Door Opening was not held until January 16, and the Omezame Festival was conducted on January 17 . In earlier times, festivals were held on January 17, the middle *Tori* day of April, July 28, September 19, and October 10, with the main annual festival on November 19 (according to *Tokusen Shinmeichō*, *Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province*, and *Meiji Shrine Records*). === History === The shrine is also known by the name from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, “Kamo Ōkami no Miko no Kami Shrine,” and is commonly called “Kamo Shrine.” It also has the following former names: * “Kamobe-no-Miya” (archival documents) * “Kamo Daimyōjin” (*Hitachi Nijūhakkō*) * “Kamo Miko Shrine” (*Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names*, *Newly Selected Scenic Gazetteer*) * “Haguro Shrine” (*Newly Selected Scenic Gazetteer*) The shrine grounds are located on a hill, which is called Mount Kamo . According to tradition, the shrine was formerly a unified complex of three shrines—Upper, Middle, and Lower—enshrining Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami, Ōji-no-Mikoto, and Kōso-no-Mikoto respectively. However, this tradition does not appear in prewar shrine registers or documents such as Meiji-period local gazetteers, shrine records, *Meiji Shrine Records*, or in the histories distributed by the shrine itself. === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, in the 8th year of {{jalink|神武天皇|Q200188|Emperor Jinmu}} (652 BCE), a divided spirit from {{jalink|賀茂御祖神社|Q701620|Shimogamo Shrine}} in {{jalink|京都|Q740246|Kyoto}} was enshrined here . The *Meiji Shrine Records* state that “the year of foundation is unknown.” The *Jōzan Sōsui* (tourist gazetteer) states: “It was founded during the {{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|Daidō}} era (806–810) and enshrines a divided spirit from the Kamo Shrine of Kyoto.” === Accounts in the Six National Histories === In *{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*, under {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 (850), it is recorded that “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami” was placed under official shrine administration. Likewise, in Kashō 4 ({{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 1), first month (851), it is stated that all deities under heaven, regardless of whether they already held rank or not, were conferred Senior Sixth Rank, Upper. Furthermore, *{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}* records that on the 23rd day of the 9th month of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 3 (861), “Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Tamagami” was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper. Such records exist. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 (927), it is listed as one shrine (a minor shrine) among the three shrines of Niihari District, Hitachi Province, under the name “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine.” Between the divine names or shrine names—“Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi **Tama**gami” in *Montoku Jitsuroku*, “Tamagami” in *Sandai Jitsuroku*, and “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” in the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*—there are contradictory differences. If *Montoku Jitsuroku* is correct, then the divine name in *Sandai Jitsuroku* is abbreviated, and the shrine name in the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lacks the character *tama* (玉). If *Sandai Jitsuroku* and the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* are both correct and refer to different deities, then *Montoku Jitsuroku* contains a serious error in conflating two deities. This issue has long been a subject of scholarly discussion. * The theory that *Montoku Jitsuroku* contains an error ** *Jōshishi Ryō-gun Gōkō (Hitachi Province District and Village Studies)* sets up the heading “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” and states, “Upon examination of the Jitsuroku, there is an error,” asserting that *Montoku Jitsuroku* is mistaken. In the section on “Nunushi-ishi Shrine,” it further says: “Upon examining both Jitsuroku, where it is written ‘Nunushi Tama,’ this may be an error of the Jinmyōchō; moreover, the original text of Montoku reads ‘By imperial edict, Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami,’ recording that two shrines were simultaneously made official shrines. According to the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935|Chronicles of Emperor Sujin}}, it seems that the shrine priest should be Nunushi Tama.” It thus holds that both the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* and *Montoku Jitsuroku* contain errors. That is, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* should have written “Nunushi Tama Shrine” but instead wrote “Nunushi Ishi Shrine,” and *Montoku Jitsuroku* should have recorded that two seats—“Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” and “Nunushi Tamagami”—were listed as official shrines, but instead recorded a single seat, “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami.” As evidence, it cites passages from the Sujin Tennō chronicle of the *Nihon Shoki*, which are also relevant to considerations of the enshrined deity. ** *{{jalink|Dai Nihon Chimei Jisho|Q17213149}}* adopts the theory of the *Gun-gōkō* and states, “Properly this should be the Nunushi-ishi deity; because stone was regarded as the divine body, it was also called Nunushi Tama,” suggesting that while Nunushi-ishi Shrine is the correct shrine name, the honorific name Nunushi Tamagami may also have existed. ** In the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album, the history of Nunushi-ishi Shrine makes no mention of Nunushi Tamagami. However, the current Nunushi-ishi Shrine, in its precinct information board, treats the descriptions in *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku* as referring to this shrine. On the other hand, its tradition of foundation states that it has long been revered as “Nunushi-ishi Daimyōjin,” and there is no indication of the existence of an honorific name Nunushi Tamagami as suggested by the place-name dictionary. ** In the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album, the history of {{jalink|Ōkunidama Shrine (Sakuragawa City)|Q11433665|Ōkunidama Shrine}} mentions *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku*. However, identifying Ōkunidama with Nunushi Tamagami is not seen in other shrine records or local gazetteers. Moreover, “Ōkunidama” is recorded in *{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}* as having been promoted from unranked to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 12 (845), and subsequently, in the general promotion of deities in Kashō 4 (Ninju 1) (851), it was promoted to at least Junior Fifth Rank, Upper. Therefore, it is difficult to consider it the same deity as the “Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Nunushi Tamagami” of *Sandai Jitsuroku* dated Jōgan 3, 9th month, 23rd day (861). * The theory that *Montoku Jitsuroku* contains no error ** Various works such as *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi*, *Tokusen Jinmyōchō*, *Jingishi Ryō*, and *{{jalink|Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708}}* hold that there is no doubt that “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami” in *Montoku Jitsuroku* and “Tamagami” in *Sandai Jitsuroku* are the correct divine names, and that the shrine name in the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* contains an omission. *Jinja Kakuroku*, in its heading, supplements the missing character and gives “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi **Tama**gami Shrine.” ** *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* sets up the heading “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” and records the descriptions from *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku* as the history of this shrine. However, it does not list the divine name Nunushi Tamagami among the enshrined deities. At present, the shrines proposed as candidates for identification with the “Tamagami” of *Sandai Jitsuroku* are Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami Shrine, Nunushi-ishi Shrine (Ōwada, {{jalink|Hokota City|Q857297}}), and Ōkunidama Shrine (Ōkunidama, {{jalink|Sakuragawa City|Q990350}}). Nothing added, nothing removed. --- === About the enshrined deities === At present, the shrine enshrines three deities: Betsuraime-no-Mikoto, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto, and Nushitama-no-Kami, but historically there have been various theories. * **Kamo Daimyōjin** ** *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* states, “According to the transmitted tradition of this shrine, it enshrines Kamo Daimyōjin,” and records a shrine tradition that worships Kamo Daimyōjin. ** In *Suifushiryō*, it is written, “{{jalink|水戸市|Q204249|Mito}}’s {{jalink|神応寺 (水戸市)|Q17217908|Jin’ō-ji}} is also said to have transferred this shrine,” and records a tradition that this shrine was the original sanctuary of Betsuraikō-ōmikami (Motoyama-chō, Mito City), which had been the tutelary shrine of Jin’ō-ji. However, this conflicts with the origin legend of Jin’ō-ji and the shrine tradition of Betsuraikō-ōmikami. * **Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto** ** *Jinja Kakuroku* records a shrine tradition stating, “At the place of enshrinement, in Nakagun Shōkamobe Village, now Kamo Village, the enshrined deity is called Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto.” ** *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō*, while acknowledging the shrine tradition that enshrines Kamo Daimyōjin, argues based on the account in the Chronicle of Emperor Sujin that there should be no doubt that the “Miko-shinshu” refers to Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto. In the Sujin Chronicle, it is recorded that, following a divine oracle ({{jalink|神託|Q217123}}) of {{jalink|大物主|Q10936701|Ōmononushi Ōkami}}, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto (also called Ofutataneko-no-Mikoto), said to be the child of Ōmononushi Ōkami and {{jalink|タマヨリビメ|Q56344982|Ikutama-yoribime}}, was appointed as the priest of {{jalink|大神神社|Q705542}} (Ōmiwa no Ōkami), whereupon an epidemic ceased. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|古事記|Q813031|Kojiki}}, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto is described as the ancestor of the Miwa clan (Miwano-kimi) and the Kamo clan (Kamono-kimi). ** *Gunkyōkō* supplements *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* by stating: “There is a nearby Ōta Village; could this shrine be its origin? Ōta appears in a document of {{jalink|足利基氏|Q4306921|Ashikaga Motouji}} from {{jalink|明徳|Q1145032|Meitoku}} 2 (1391) in the {{jalink|新編鎌倉志|Q2397169|Shinpen Kamakura-shi}}, and is now renamed Inuta,” suggesting that the “Ōta” in “Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto” may have shifted to Ōta Village west of Kamobe Village (present-day Inuta, Sakuragawa City). * **Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto** ** *Tokusen Shimmeichō* records a shrine tradition stating, “According to shrine tradition, the enshrined deities are Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto,” indicating the worship of these two deities. * **Nushitama-no-Kami** ** *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* critically introduces the theory of *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō*, stating: “Ōtaneneko was indeed the priest of Ōmononushi, but he was not the priest of the Kamo deity.” Regarding the “Kamo deity,” it suggests either {{jalink|事代主|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}}, listed as three seats at {{jalink|鴨都波神社|Q11675614|Kamo-tsuha Yae Kotoshironushi Shrine}} in {{jalink|大和国|Q907495|Yamato Province}}{{jalink|葛上郡|Q11620858|Katsukami District}}, or Betsuraime-no-Kami of {{jalink|賀茂別雷神社|Q700448}} in {{jalink|山城国|Q749276|Yamashiro Province}}{{jalink|愛宕郡|Q10981345|Atago District}}, or possibly {{jalink|賀茂建角身命|Q11634939|Kamo-taketsunumi-no-Mikoto}}, one of the two seats at Kamo Mioya Shrine, concluding that it is difficult to decide which. Nevertheless, regardless of interpretation, it states that since the deity is clearly named “Nushitama-no-Kami” in the *Sandai Jitsuroku*, there is no reason to doubt that the deity resides as Nushitama-no-Kami. ** *Jingishiryō* (by the supplementary author of *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi*) states, “Presumably, it enshrines Nushitama-no-Kami, who resides as the child deity of the Great Kamo Deity,” and suggests that the shrine name means “Nushitama-no-Kami, the child deity of the Great Kamo Deity.” ** *Jinja Kakuroku*, supplementing the shrine name with the character for “tama,” renders it as “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine” and states that “the enshrined deity is clear.” ** *Tokusen Shimmeichō* rejects the shrine tradition, stating: “The tradition that the enshrined deities are Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto appears to be based on the Sujin Chronicle, which records that Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto was appointed as priest to worship Miwa Ōkami, but the Kamo deity is not Miwa Ōkami; furthermore, Betsuraime-no-Mikoto is said to be the child of the Kamo deity, but this too is merely a shrine selection, and thus is not adopted here,” concluding that the enshrined deity is a single seat of Nushitama-no-Kami. In the Sujin Chronicle, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōmononushi Ōkami and Ikutama-yoribime-no-Mikoto, and is not connected to the Kamo deity. *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* and subsequent works criticize this point and argue that one should straightforwardly follow the divine names recorded in the *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku*. In other genealogies, in the {{jalink|先代旧事本紀|Q2716751|Sendai Kujihongi}}, Ōtataneko-no-Mikoto is described as the grandchild deity of Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto and Tsumishihaye Kotoshironushi-no-Kami, the child of Ōkuninushi-no-Kami. In the fragmentary Yamashiro Province *Fudoki*, {{jalink|タマヨリビメ|Q56344982|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} (Kamo Tamayorihime, the Tamayorihime of the {{jalink|賀茂県主氏|Q2975287|Kamo clan}}) is the parent deity of Kamo Betsuraime-no-Mikoto, but Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto does not appear. Note that these {{jalink|タマヨリビメ|Q56344982|Tamayorihime}} are different goddesses who share the same name and are female. The “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama-no-Kami” of the *Montoku Jitsuroku*, the “Nushitama-no-Kami” of the *Sandai Jitsuroku*, and the “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi Shrine” of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* are all written as referring to a single seat deity. This holds true even if the *Montoku Jitsuroku* wording is regarded as a scribal error for “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi-no-Kami.” Therefore, at least by the time of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is considered that the deity listed as a government shrine and granted divine rank was a single seat. In the early modern period, the shrine was regarded as a shrine of “Kamo Daimyōjin.” By the end of the {{jalink|江戸|Q215646|Edo}} period, the interpretation of “Mikogami Nushi” as described in *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* had spread, and it is thought that the shrine tradition solidified as either enshrining Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto alone, or enshrining two seats together with Betsuraime-no-Mikoto. However, it is also possible that Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto had already been enshrined as part of the Kamo Daimyōjin cult, and that *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* attempted a theoretical explanation (which was not fully resolved). In the late {{jalink|明治|Q3125096|Meiji}} period, the *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* lists two enshrined deities—Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto—based on the shrine tradition at that time. In the {{jalink|昭和|Q327567|Shōwa}} period *Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album*, the shrine name is given as “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine,” with “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama-no-Kami” added as the principal deity, and the previously enshrined two deities becoming subsidiary deities. Both the *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* and the *Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album* explicitly cite the {{jalink|神社明細帳|Q11590704|Jinja Meisaichō}} as their source, so it is thought that a formal transition from “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi Shrine” to “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine” occurred between the publication dates of these two works. However, in neither transition were the deities enshrined at the time removed. Absolutely — same treatment as before. Below is a **faithful, formatting-preserving translation**, with **all headings, lists, templates, refs, gallery blocks, punctuation, and wiki markup kept intact**. No interpretation added. --- === Before the Medieval Period === In {{jalink|和銅|Q1373026}} 4 (711), the Kamo Festival was conducted for the first time. In {{jalink|寛平|Q1346292}} 9 (897), a {{jalink|臨時祭|Q11612499|temporary festival}} was performed for the first time. Thereafter, imperial envoys were dispatched on several occasions. In {{jalink|永承|Q1193549}} 5 (1050), shrine priests were appointed for the first time. In {{jalink|康平|Q1193572}} 5 (1062), when {{jalink|源頼義|Q3082851|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} visited Hitachi Province under imperial command to subjugate {{jalink|安倍貞任|Q3616500|Abe no Sadatō}}, he prayed here for victory in battle. In the 6th year of Kōhei, 6th month (1063), Minamoto no Yoriyoshi repaired the shrine buildings as an act of thanksgiving for victory. Regarding the date of this thanksgiving, *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* records Kōhei 6, 8th month (1063); *Gunkyōkō* records {{jalink|治暦|Q934365}} 4, yi-si year, 8th month (1068); and *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* records Chiryaku 4, 8th month (1068). The latter further notes that, according to the ridgepole inscription, one entire village of Kamo was allocated as offerings land for the deity. In {{jalink|元久|Q1194276}} 1 (1204), by the decree titled “Toba-in, Shirakawa, and Go-Shirakawa’s oath,” the shrine was designated “Nakagun-shō Kamobe-miya” and exempted from tribute rice for the Daijōsai ({{jalink|段銭|Q11547088}}), labor duties, and various miscellaneous {{jalink|公事|Q11391888|public obligations}}. In {{jalink|貞永|Q1194168}} 1, 11th month (1232), Minamoto no Moriyoshi was appointed shrine priest of “Kamo in Nakagun, Hitachi Province”. *Gunkyōkō* suggests that “Kamo-no-suke Munemi,” appearing in a Kumano-yama vow document from {{jalink|明徳|Q1145032}} 3 (1392), and “Kamobe Kamo-no-kami,” appearing in the execution register following the fall of {{jalink|結城城|Q11606565|Yūki Castle}} in {{jalink|嘉吉|Q1193238}} 1 (1441), were likely shrine officials of this shrine. In {{jalink|元亨|Q509889}} 3 (1323), {{jalink|後醍醐天皇|Q313942|Emperor Go-Daigo}} donated a copy of the {{jalink|大般若経|Q15905785|Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra}} bearing his {{jalink|諱|Q64026473|personal name}} written at the end of the scroll. The sutras preserved at the shrine were donated over a period from {{jalink|正安|Q1327427}} 1, 1st month, 28th day (1299) through {{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}} 5 (1677); among these, 46 volumes bear colophons stating “donated by Emperor Go-Daigo”. In {{jalink|元弘|Q1194556}} 3, 3rd month (1333), a document states: “All enemies of the court, including Takatoki-hōshi ({{jalink|北条高時|Q2355334|Hōjō Takatoki}}), and their associates, shall not contest the lands currently held” (Kamo Shrine Documents), whereby Emperor Go-Daigo confirmed the shrine’s land holdings. In {{jalink|文和|Q1146252}} 3, 9th month (1354), through the efforts of Shinozawa-in head monk Shunen and others since Emperor Go-Daigo, a complete set of 600 volumes of the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra was assembled. Of these, 336 volumes designated as cultural properties survive, and 301 volumes have confirmed numbering. Most volumes record this era name in their colophons. During the {{jalink|応永|Q1193078}} era (1394–1428), {{jalink|佐竹貞義|Q11383481|Satake Sadayoshi}} donated copies of the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra bearing his name at the end of the scrolls. In {{jalink|応永|Q1193078}} 21 (1414), {{jalink|佐竹義盛|Q11383456|Satake Yoshimori}} rebuilt the shrine buildings, appointing {{jalink|宍戸氏|Q11451793|Shishido Shirō Yoshitoshi}} and Noda Saburō as construction magistrates, and carved the Satake clan crest, the “Five-ribbed Sun Disc” (five-ribbed folding fan with sun disc), into the ridgepole (ridge tag). Some sources attribute this construction solely to a donation by Shishido Yoshitoshi. In {{jalink|永享|Q1193059}} 12 (1440), during the {{jalink|結城合戦|Q11606564|Yūki War}}, {{jalink|足利持氏|Q4805073|Ashikaga Mochiuji}}’s son {{jalink|足利安王丸|Q21019758|Ashikaga Yasumaru-ō}} offered a vow document and, as thanksgiving, donated one district within the county as shrine rice fields. In {{jalink|永禄|Q1143341}} 12 (1569), {{jalink|佐竹義重 (十八代当主)|Q1133798|Satake Yoshishige}} rebuilt the current main sanctuary (as indicated by the ridge tag). It is also said that {{jalink|佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)|Q1071525|Satake Yoshinobu}}’s coming-of-age ceremony (“Nabiro-me,” {{jalink|元服|Q138321|genpuku}}) was held at this shrine. The area where accompanying retainers rested was called “Satake-nohara,” a place name that survived until the end of the Edo period. === After the Early Modern Period === In {{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}} 7 (1602), the Tokugawa shogunate donated three koku of shrine land, citing established precedent (extant documents). During the {{jalink|正保|Q1146192}} era (1645–1648), {{jalink|笠間城|Q11599983|Kasama Castle}} lord {{jalink|井上正利|Q11373071|Inoue Kawachi-no-kami Masatoshi}} and others dedicated plaques bearing the shrine name. In {{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}} 5 (1677), villagers Adachi Sōzaemon and Adachi Yahyōe of Kamobe Village repaired the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra. When the {{jalink|三河牧野氏|Q2975316|Makino clan}} entered {{jalink|笠間藩|Q3034461|Kasama Domain}}, the shrine became the tutelary shrine of forty-two villages and received ten bundles of rice as ritual offerings. In {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}} 3 (1870), {{jalink|笠間藩|Q3034461|Kasama Domain}} governor {{jalink|牧野貞直|Q10860149|Makino Sadanao}} erected a stone monument inscribed with a poem by {{jalink|葉室光俊|Q23613350|Udaiben Mitsutoshi}}: “**Aoi grass— the many-leaved mountain shrine of Kamo; familiar now, at the season of sacred renewal, how splendid the capital must be.**” In Meiji 6 (1873), under the modern shrine ranking system, the shrine was classified as a village shrine. On June 4, Meiji 12 (1879), it was promoted to the rank of township shrine. On March 7, 1966 (Shōwa 41), 336 volumes of the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra were designated as Tangible Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture (calligraphic works). In November 1979 (Shōwa 54), Makoto Nakata, mayor of {{jalink|岩瀬町|Q11475420|Iwase Town}}, oversaw repairs to the shrine buildingsFrom the commemorative碑 of shrine rebuilding and repairs within the precincts.. On May 12, 1980 (Shōwa 55), one main sanctuary building of Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property (structure) of Iwase Town. === Shrine Rank === === Divine Rank === **Hitachi Province — Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama-no-Kami** * {{jalink|嘉祥|Q1373157}} 3 (851), 6th month – Listed as an official shrine『{{jalink|日本文徳天皇実録|Q1052905}}』《卷一 嘉祥三年（八五〇）六月己酉【三】》○己酉。雷震西寺刹柱。剥取其竿。中央一許丈。去落於右馬頭藤原朝臣春津宅。詔以武藏國廣瀬神。常陸國鴨大神御子神主玉神。並列於官社。安房國國造正八位上伴直千福麻呂授外從五位下。 **Nushitama-no-Kami** * {{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 3 (862), 9th month – Junior Fifth Rank, Upper『{{jalink|日本三代実録|Q1052981}}』《卷五 貞觀三年（八六一）九月廿三日甲午》○廿三日甲午。授常陸國從五位下主玉神從五位上。 *Gunkyōkō* states that by the Meiō era (1492–1501) the deity had reached Senior Second Rank, and *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* records a subsequent promotion to Senior First Rank. === Modern Period === * {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}} 6 (1873) – Village shrine * June 4, Meiji 12 (1879) – Township shrine === Cultural Properties === === Ibaraki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * {{jalink|大般若波羅蜜多経|Q15905785|Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra}} — 336 volumes (designated March 7, Shōwa 40) === Sakuragawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main sanctuary of Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine === Shrine Precincts === * **Living Torii** ** Instead of a {{jalink|鳥居|Q234731|torii}}, sacred ropes are hung directly on cedar trees. Although torii originally stood here, it is said that after Minamoto no Yoriyoshi displayed the severed head of a defeated mountain hag on the torii as a warning, villagers feared the pollution and her vengeful spirit and dismantled the torii. File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Torii1.JPG|First living torii File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Torii2.JPG|Second living torii File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Torii3.JPG|Third living torii * **Worship Hall, Main Sanctuary** File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Main hall.JPG|Worship hall File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Worship Hall.JPG|Main sanctuary File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Main hall Tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kanameishi.JPG|Kaname-ishi (keystone) === Access === * 2 km east of {{jalink|羽黒駅 (茨城県)|Q5638843|Haguro Station}} on the {{jalink|水戸線|Q302388|Mito Line}} of {{jalink|東日本旅客鉄道|Q499071|JR East}}, about a 15-minute walk === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2|refs= Regarding the reading of the shrine name, according to the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Agency registry: *Jinja Kakuroku* reads it as “kamonoōkami mikokaminushitama,” *Jingishiryō* as “kamonoōkami no mikokaminushitama-no,” and *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* (as Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi Shrine) as “kamonoōkami no mikokaminushi-no.” At least in earlier times, “Kamo Ōkami” was read as “kamonoōkami.” According to a shrine history pamphlet distributed by Isobe Inamura Shrine (administrative shrine). A pamphlet post appears among the shrine items on the “[[https://omairi.club/](https://omairi.club/) Omairi]” page for this shrine (viewed April 2020). *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi*, section on shrines. *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* includes a copy of an old document dated Ken’nin 4, 3rd month, 7th day (1204). *Gunkyōkō* gives Ken’nin 3 (1203), stating “a shrine of imperial decree.” *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* includes a copy of an old document dated Teinei 1, 11th month, 21st day (1232). *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* includes a copy of an old document dated Genkō 3, 7th month, 26th day (1333). *Hitachi Shiryō Gunkyōkō* (Hitachi Province District and Village Study). The origin legend of Jin’ō-ji states it is a divided spirit of the Raijin Shrine of {{jalink|大阪|Q11439906|Osaka}}. “Osaka” here refers to Ōsaka outside {{jalink|水戸城|Q848997|Mito Castle}} (Kitami-chō, {{jalink|水戸市|Q204249|Mito City}}). According to the shrine tradition of Betsuraikō-ōmikami, the shrine was founded in {{jalink|神亀|Q1373210}} 1 (724), enshrining Betsuraime-no-Mikoto. Originally located in Ōsaka and called Ōsaka Daimyōjin, it was relocated by Satake Yoshinobu to Takajō-machi (around Minami-machi, Mito City) under Jin’ō-ji’s administration, and later relocated again by {{jalink|徳川光圀|Q1194944|Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} (Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album). Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Property Commentary. Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album. *Jōzan Sōsui*. }} === References === * [[https://www.ndl.go.jp/](https://www.ndl.go.jp/) National Diet Library]: Viewable via the [[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/](https://dl.ndl.go.jp/) National Diet Library Digital Collection]. ** Komiyama Masahide, “Suifushiryō, vol. 25.” {{jalink|文化 (元号)|Q1373146|Bunka}} 4 (1807). ** Suzuka Tsuranari et al., “Jinja Kakuroku.” Meiji 3 (1870). ** Nakayama Nobuna, Kurita Hiroshi (eds.), “Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi, lower volume.” Sekizenkansha. Meiji 32–34 (1899–1901). ** Yoshida Tōgo, “Dai Nihon Chimei Jiten, lower volume, 2nd ed.” Fuzanbō. October 17, Meiji 40 (1907). ** Yanagisawa Tsurukichi, “Jōzan Sōsui Meishō Koseki.” Ryūtantō Tokyo Branch. October, Meiji 41 (1908). ** Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office (ed.), “Meiji Jinja Shiryō: Prefectural and Township Shrines (Upper).” Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office. Meiji 45 (1912). ** Ministry of Doctrine (ed.), “Tokusen Shimmeichō.” {{jalink|大正|Q329174|Taishō}} 14 (1925). Isobe Kōyōdō. ** Ibaraki Shimbun, “Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album.” Ibaraki Shimbunsha. Shōwa 16 (1942). * Miyamoto Mototama, “Hitachi Shiryō Gunkyōkō (Hitachi Province District and Village Study).” Man’en 1 (1860). Available via the [[https://www.nijl.ac.jp/](https://www.nijl.ac.jp/) National Institute of Japanese Literature]. Viewed April 2020. * [[https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/](https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/) Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education], “[[https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/bunkazai/ken/syoseki/5-14/5-14.html](https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/bunkazai/ken/syoseki/5-14/5-14.html) Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra, 336 volumes].” Cultural Property Commentary. Viewed April 2020. * [[http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/index.html](http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/index.html) Sakuragawa City], “[[http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/page/dir001739.html](http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/page/dir001739.html) Cultural Properties].” Cultural Property List. Viewed April 2020. {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{デフォルトソート:かもおおかみみこかみぬしたましんしや}} --- If you want, next I can: * normalize this into **academic prose English**, or * keep the markup but **simplify for readability**, or * align terminology across both translated sections for **consistency auditing** (which I suspect you care about 👀). == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:加茂神社]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:茨城県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

Kamookamimikokaminushitama-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県桜川市加茂部694', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Kamookamimikokaminushitama-jinja}} [[JA:鴨大神御子神主玉神社]] {{wikidata link|Q66085127}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushitama Shrine |Image = [[File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Main hall.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}{{jalink|Sakuragawa City|Q990350}}Kamobe 694 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Nushitama-no-Kami}} |Shintai = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 8th year of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Chief priest = |Main sanctuary = {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other names = Kamo Shrine |Pilgrimage affiliation = |Annual festival = November 14–15 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushitama Shrine''' (かもおおかみみこかみぬしたまじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine enshrining Nushitama-no-Kami, located along the Sakuragawa River in Kamobe, Sakuragawa City, Ibaraki Prefecture. It is identified with the Shikinaisha (minor shrine) “Kamo Ōkami Mikokaminushi Shrine” of Niihari District in Hitachi Province. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was classified as a gōsha (village shrine). {{Double image aside|right|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Hiejin.JPG|160|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kashima.JPG|160|{{center|Auxiliary shrine: Hie Shrine}}|{{center|Auxiliary shrine: Kashima Shrine}}}} {{Triple image aside|right|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kasuga.JPG|120|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kotohira.JPG|120|Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Hachiman.JPG|120|{{center|Sub-shrine: Kasuga Shrine}}|{{center|Sub-shrine: Kotohira Shrine}}|{{center|Sub-shrine: Hachiman Shrine}}}} === Enshrined Deities === * {{jalink|賀茂別雷命|Q11634943|別雷神}} (Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami) * {{jalink|大田田根子|Q55536798|大田々根子神}} (Ōtatane-ko-no-Kami) * {{jalink|主玉神}} (Nushitama-no-Kami) In {{jalink|府県郷社明治神社誌料|Q11486103|Meiji Shrine Records}}, Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto and Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami are listed as the two enshrined deities. In the *Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album*, the principal enshrined deity is Kamo Ōkami no Miko no Kami Nushitama-no-Kami, with Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto and Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto as associated deities. === Shrine Precinct Shrines === ==== Subsidiary Shrines ==== * Hie Shrine – (principal enshrined deity) Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto * Kashima Shrine – (principal enshrined deity) Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto ==== Minor Shrines ==== * Ōsugi Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Kotohira Shrine (Konpira Shrine) * Tenmangū (Tenman Ōkami) * Hakusan Shrine * Kasuga Shrine * Inari Shrine In {{jalink|府県郷社明治神社誌料|Q11486103|Meiji Shrine Records}}, eight shrines are listed: Hie Shrine, Kashima Shrine, Inari Shrine, Kasuga Shrine, Tenmangū, Hakusan Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Kotohira Shrine. === Festivals === * Omezame Festival (January 1) * National Foundation Festival (February 11) * Gion Festival (July 24–26) * Grand Festival (November 14–15) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Winter Solstice Festival (around December 22) * Oyasumi Festival and First Door Opening (December 31) Notice board at the shrine precincts: Annual Events. Until around 1985 (Shōwa 60), the First Door Opening was not held until January 16, and the Omezame Festival was conducted on January 17 . In earlier times, festivals were held on January 17, the middle *Tori* day of April, July 28, September 19, and October 10, with the main annual festival on November 19 (according to *Tokusen Shinmeichō*, *Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province*, and *Meiji Shrine Records*). === History === The shrine is also known by the name from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, “Kamo Ōkami no Miko no Kami Shrine,” and is commonly called “Kamo Shrine.” It also has the following former names: * “Kamobe-no-Miya” (archival documents) * “Kamo Daimyōjin” (*Hitachi Nijūhakkō*) * “Kamo Miko Shrine” (*Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names*, *Newly Selected Scenic Gazetteer*) * “Haguro Shrine” (*Newly Selected Scenic Gazetteer*) The shrine grounds are located on a hill, which is called Mount Kamo . According to tradition, the shrine was formerly a unified complex of three shrines—Upper, Middle, and Lower—enshrining Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami, Ōji-no-Mikoto, and Kōso-no-Mikoto respectively. However, this tradition does not appear in prewar shrine registers or documents such as Meiji-period local gazetteers, shrine records, *Meiji Shrine Records*, or in the histories distributed by the shrine itself. === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, in the 8th year of {{jalink|神武天皇|Q200188|Emperor Jinmu}} (652 BCE), a divided spirit from {{jalink|賀茂御祖神社|Q701620|Shimogamo Shrine}} in {{jalink|京都|Q740246|Kyoto}} was enshrined here . The *Meiji Shrine Records* state that “the year of foundation is unknown.” The *Jōzan Sōsui* (tourist gazetteer) states: “It was founded during the {{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|Daidō}} era (806–810) and enshrines a divided spirit from the Kamo Shrine of Kyoto.” === Accounts in the Six National Histories === In *{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*, under {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 (850), it is recorded that “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami” was placed under official shrine administration. Likewise, in Kashō 4 ({{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 1), first month (851), it is stated that all deities under heaven, regardless of whether they already held rank or not, were conferred Senior Sixth Rank, Upper. Furthermore, *{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}* records that on the 23rd day of the 9th month of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 3 (861), “Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Tamagami” was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper. Such records exist. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 (927), it is listed as one shrine (a minor shrine) among the three shrines of Niihari District, Hitachi Province, under the name “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine.” Between the divine names or shrine names—“Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi **Tama**gami” in *Montoku Jitsuroku*, “Tamagami” in *Sandai Jitsuroku*, and “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” in the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*—there are contradictory differences. If *Montoku Jitsuroku* is correct, then the divine name in *Sandai Jitsuroku* is abbreviated, and the shrine name in the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lacks the character *tama* (玉). If *Sandai Jitsuroku* and the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* are both correct and refer to different deities, then *Montoku Jitsuroku* contains a serious error in conflating two deities. This issue has long been a subject of scholarly discussion. * The theory that *Montoku Jitsuroku* contains an error ** *Jōshishi Ryō-gun Gōkō (Hitachi Province District and Village Studies)* sets up the heading “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” and states, “Upon examination of the Jitsuroku, there is an error,” asserting that *Montoku Jitsuroku* is mistaken. In the section on “Nunushi-ishi Shrine,” it further says: “Upon examining both Jitsuroku, where it is written ‘Nunushi Tama,’ this may be an error of the Jinmyōchō; moreover, the original text of Montoku reads ‘By imperial edict, Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami,’ recording that two shrines were simultaneously made official shrines. According to the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935|Chronicles of Emperor Sujin}}, it seems that the shrine priest should be Nunushi Tama.” It thus holds that both the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* and *Montoku Jitsuroku* contain errors. That is, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* should have written “Nunushi Tama Shrine” but instead wrote “Nunushi Ishi Shrine,” and *Montoku Jitsuroku* should have recorded that two seats—“Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” and “Nunushi Tamagami”—were listed as official shrines, but instead recorded a single seat, “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami.” As evidence, it cites passages from the Sujin Tennō chronicle of the *Nihon Shoki*, which are also relevant to considerations of the enshrined deity. ** *{{jalink|Dai Nihon Chimei Jisho|Q17213149}}* adopts the theory of the *Gun-gōkō* and states, “Properly this should be the Nunushi-ishi deity; because stone was regarded as the divine body, it was also called Nunushi Tama,” suggesting that while Nunushi-ishi Shrine is the correct shrine name, the honorific name Nunushi Tamagami may also have existed. ** In the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album, the history of Nunushi-ishi Shrine makes no mention of Nunushi Tamagami. However, the current Nunushi-ishi Shrine, in its precinct information board, treats the descriptions in *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku* as referring to this shrine. On the other hand, its tradition of foundation states that it has long been revered as “Nunushi-ishi Daimyōjin,” and there is no indication of the existence of an honorific name Nunushi Tamagami as suggested by the place-name dictionary. ** In the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album, the history of {{jalink|Ōkunidama Shrine (Sakuragawa City)|Q11433665|Ōkunidama Shrine}} mentions *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku*. However, identifying Ōkunidama with Nunushi Tamagami is not seen in other shrine records or local gazetteers. Moreover, “Ōkunidama” is recorded in *{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}* as having been promoted from unranked to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 12 (845), and subsequently, in the general promotion of deities in Kashō 4 (Ninju 1) (851), it was promoted to at least Junior Fifth Rank, Upper. Therefore, it is difficult to consider it the same deity as the “Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Nunushi Tamagami” of *Sandai Jitsuroku* dated Jōgan 3, 9th month, 23rd day (861). * The theory that *Montoku Jitsuroku* contains no error ** Various works such as *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi*, *Tokusen Jinmyōchō*, *Jingishi Ryō*, and *{{jalink|Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708}}* hold that there is no doubt that “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami” in *Montoku Jitsuroku* and “Tamagami” in *Sandai Jitsuroku* are the correct divine names, and that the shrine name in the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* contains an omission. *Jinja Kakuroku*, in its heading, supplements the missing character and gives “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi **Tama**gami Shrine.” ** *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* sets up the heading “Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Shrine” and records the descriptions from *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku* as the history of this shrine. However, it does not list the divine name Nunushi Tamagami among the enshrined deities. At present, the shrines proposed as candidates for identification with the “Tamagami” of *Sandai Jitsuroku* are Kamo-ōmikami no Mikogami-nushi Tamagami Shrine, Nunushi-ishi Shrine (Ōwada, {{jalink|Hokota City|Q857297}}), and Ōkunidama Shrine (Ōkunidama, {{jalink|Sakuragawa City|Q990350}}). Got it — I’ll translate this **faithfully**, keeping **all formatting, list structure, punctuation, wiki-style markup, refs, and tone** intact. Nothing added, nothing removed. --- === About the enshrined deities === At present, the shrine enshrines three deities: Betsuraime-no-Mikoto, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto, and Nushitama-no-Kami, but historically there have been various theories. * **Kamo Daimyōjin** ** *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* states, “According to the transmitted tradition of this shrine, it enshrines Kamo Daimyōjin,” and records a shrine tradition that worships Kamo Daimyōjin. ** In *Suifushiryō*, it is written, “{{jalink|水戸市|Q204249|Mito}}’s {{jalink|神応寺 (水戸市)|Q17217908|Jin’ō-ji}} is also said to have transferred this shrine,” and records a tradition that this shrine was the original sanctuary of Betsuraikō-ōmikami (Motoyama-chō, Mito City), which had been the tutelary shrine of Jin’ō-ji. However, this conflicts with the origin legend of Jin’ō-ji and the shrine tradition of Betsuraikō-ōmikami. * **Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto** ** *Jinja Kakuroku* records a shrine tradition stating, “At the place of enshrinement, in Nakagun Shōkamobe Village, now Kamo Village, the enshrined deity is called Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto.” ** *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō*, while acknowledging the shrine tradition that enshrines Kamo Daimyōjin, argues based on the account in the Chronicle of Emperor Sujin that there should be no doubt that the “Miko-shinshu” refers to Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto. In the Sujin Chronicle, it is recorded that, following a divine oracle ({{jalink|神託|Q217123}}) of {{jalink|大物主|Q10936701|Ōmononushi Ōkami}}, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto (also called Ofutataneko-no-Mikoto), said to be the child of Ōmononushi Ōkami and {{jalink|タマヨリビメ|Q56344982|Ikutama-yoribime}}, was appointed as the priest of {{jalink|大神神社|Q705542}} (Ōmiwa no Ōkami), whereupon an epidemic ceased. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|古事記|Q813031|Kojiki}}, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto is described as the ancestor of the Miwa clan (Miwano-kimi) and the Kamo clan (Kamono-kimi). ** *Gunkyōkō* supplements *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* by stating: “There is a nearby Ōta Village; could this shrine be its origin? Ōta appears in a document of {{jalink|足利基氏|Q4306921|Ashikaga Motouji}} from {{jalink|明徳|Q1145032|Meitoku}} 2 (1391) in the {{jalink|新編鎌倉志|Q2397169|Shinpen Kamakura-shi}}, and is now renamed Inuta,” suggesting that the “Ōta” in “Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto” may have shifted to Ōta Village west of Kamobe Village (present-day Inuta, Sakuragawa City). * **Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto** ** *Tokusen Shimmeichō* records a shrine tradition stating, “According to shrine tradition, the enshrined deities are Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto,” indicating the worship of these two deities. * **Nushitama-no-Kami** ** *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* critically introduces the theory of *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō*, stating: “Ōtaneneko was indeed the priest of Ōmononushi, but he was not the priest of the Kamo deity.” Regarding the “Kamo deity,” it suggests either {{jalink|事代主|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}}, listed as three seats at {{jalink|鴨都波神社|Q11675614|Kamo-tsuha Yae Kotoshironushi Shrine}} in {{jalink|大和国|Q907495|Yamato Province}}{{jalink|葛上郡|Q11620858|Katsukami District}}, or Betsuraime-no-Kami of {{jalink|賀茂別雷神社|Q700448}} in {{jalink|山城国|Q749276|Yamashiro Province}}{{jalink|愛宕郡|Q10981345|Atago District}}, or possibly {{jalink|賀茂建角身命|Q11634939|Kamo-taketsunumi-no-Mikoto}}, one of the two seats at Kamo Mioya Shrine, concluding that it is difficult to decide which. Nevertheless, regardless of interpretation, it states that since the deity is clearly named “Nushitama-no-Kami” in the *Sandai Jitsuroku*, there is no reason to doubt that the deity resides as Nushitama-no-Kami. ** *Jingishiryō* (by the supplementary author of *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi*) states, “Presumably, it enshrines Nushitama-no-Kami, who resides as the child deity of the Great Kamo Deity,” and suggests that the shrine name means “Nushitama-no-Kami, the child deity of the Great Kamo Deity.” ** *Jinja Kakuroku*, supplementing the shrine name with the character for “tama,” renders it as “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine” and states that “the enshrined deity is clear.” ** *Tokusen Shimmeichō* rejects the shrine tradition, stating: “The tradition that the enshrined deities are Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto appears to be based on the Sujin Chronicle, which records that Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto was appointed as priest to worship Miwa Ōkami, but the Kamo deity is not Miwa Ōkami; furthermore, Betsuraime-no-Mikoto is said to be the child of the Kamo deity, but this too is merely a shrine selection, and thus is not adopted here,” concluding that the enshrined deity is a single seat of Nushitama-no-Kami. In the Sujin Chronicle, Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōmononushi Ōkami and Ikutama-yoribime-no-Mikoto, and is not connected to the Kamo deity. *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* and subsequent works criticize this point and argue that one should straightforwardly follow the divine names recorded in the *Montoku Jitsuroku* and *Sandai Jitsuroku*. In other genealogies, in the {{jalink|先代旧事本紀|Q2716751|Sendai Kujihongi}}, Ōtataneko-no-Mikoto is described as the grandchild deity of Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto and Tsumishihaye Kotoshironushi-no-Kami, the child of Ōkuninushi-no-Kami. In the fragmentary Yamashiro Province *Fudoki*, {{jalink|タマヨリビメ|Q56344982|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} (Kamo Tamayorihime, the Tamayorihime of the {{jalink|賀茂県主氏|Q2975287|Kamo clan}}) is the parent deity of Kamo Betsuraime-no-Mikoto, but Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto does not appear. Note that these {{jalink|タマヨリビメ|Q56344982|Tamayorihime}} are different goddesses who share the same name and are female. The “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama-no-Kami” of the *Montoku Jitsuroku*, the “Nushitama-no-Kami” of the *Sandai Jitsuroku*, and the “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi Shrine” of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* are all written as referring to a single seat deity. This holds true even if the *Montoku Jitsuroku* wording is regarded as a scribal error for “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi-no-Kami.” Therefore, at least by the time of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is considered that the deity listed as a government shrine and granted divine rank was a single seat. In the early modern period, the shrine was regarded as a shrine of “Kamo Daimyōjin.” By the end of the {{jalink|江戸|Q215646|Edo}} period, the interpretation of “Mikogami Nushi” as described in *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* had spread, and it is thought that the shrine tradition solidified as either enshrining Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto alone, or enshrining two seats together with Betsuraime-no-Mikoto. However, it is also possible that Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto had already been enshrined as part of the Kamo Daimyōjin cult, and that *Hitachi Nijūhassha-kō* attempted a theoretical explanation (which was not fully resolved). In the late {{jalink|明治|Q3125096|Meiji}} period, the *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* lists two enshrined deities—Ōtaneneko-no-Mikoto and Betsuraime-no-Mikoto—based on the shrine tradition at that time. In the {{jalink|昭和|Q327567|Shōwa}} period *Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album*, the shrine name is given as “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine,” with “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama-no-Kami” added as the principal deity, and the previously enshrined two deities becoming subsidiary deities. Both the *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* and the *Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album* explicitly cite the {{jalink|神社明細帳|Q11590704|Jinja Meisaichō}} as their source, so it is thought that a formal transition from “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi Shrine” to “Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine” occurred between the publication dates of these two works. However, in neither transition were the deities enshrined at the time removed. Absolutely — same treatment as before. Below is a **faithful, formatting-preserving translation**, with **all headings, lists, templates, refs, gallery blocks, punctuation, and wiki markup kept intact**. No interpretation added. --- === Before the Medieval Period === In {{jalink|和銅|Q1373026}} 4 (711), the Kamo Festival was conducted for the first time. In {{jalink|寛平|Q1346292}} 9 (897), a {{jalink|臨時祭|Q11612499|temporary festival}} was performed for the first time. Thereafter, imperial envoys were dispatched on several occasions. In {{jalink|永承|Q1193549}} 5 (1050), shrine priests were appointed for the first time. In {{jalink|康平|Q1193572}} 5 (1062), when {{jalink|源頼義|Q3082851|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} visited Hitachi Province under imperial command to subjugate {{jalink|安倍貞任|Q3616500|Abe no Sadatō}}, he prayed here for victory in battle. In the 6th year of Kōhei, 6th month (1063), Minamoto no Yoriyoshi repaired the shrine buildings as an act of thanksgiving for victory. Regarding the date of this thanksgiving, *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* records Kōhei 6, 8th month (1063); *Gunkyōkō* records {{jalink|治暦|Q934365}} 4, yi-si year, 8th month (1068); and *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* records Chiryaku 4, 8th month (1068). The latter further notes that, according to the ridgepole inscription, one entire village of Kamo was allocated as offerings land for the deity. In {{jalink|元久|Q1194276}} 1 (1204), by the decree titled “Toba-in, Shirakawa, and Go-Shirakawa’s oath,” the shrine was designated “Nakagun-shō Kamobe-miya” and exempted from tribute rice for the Daijōsai ({{jalink|段銭|Q11547088}}), labor duties, and various miscellaneous {{jalink|公事|Q11391888|public obligations}}. In {{jalink|貞永|Q1194168}} 1, 11th month (1232), Minamoto no Moriyoshi was appointed shrine priest of “Kamo in Nakagun, Hitachi Province”. *Gunkyōkō* suggests that “Kamo-no-suke Munemi,” appearing in a Kumano-yama vow document from {{jalink|明徳|Q1145032}} 3 (1392), and “Kamobe Kamo-no-kami,” appearing in the execution register following the fall of {{jalink|結城城|Q11606565|Yūki Castle}} in {{jalink|嘉吉|Q1193238}} 1 (1441), were likely shrine officials of this shrine. In {{jalink|元亨|Q509889}} 3 (1323), {{jalink|後醍醐天皇|Q313942|Emperor Go-Daigo}} donated a copy of the {{jalink|大般若経|Q15905785|Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra}} bearing his {{jalink|諱|Q64026473|personal name}} written at the end of the scroll. The sutras preserved at the shrine were donated over a period from {{jalink|正安|Q1327427}} 1, 1st month, 28th day (1299) through {{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}} 5 (1677); among these, 46 volumes bear colophons stating “donated by Emperor Go-Daigo”. In {{jalink|元弘|Q1194556}} 3, 3rd month (1333), a document states: “All enemies of the court, including Takatoki-hōshi ({{jalink|北条高時|Q2355334|Hōjō Takatoki}}), and their associates, shall not contest the lands currently held” (Kamo Shrine Documents), whereby Emperor Go-Daigo confirmed the shrine’s land holdings. In {{jalink|文和|Q1146252}} 3, 9th month (1354), through the efforts of Shinozawa-in head monk Shunen and others since Emperor Go-Daigo, a complete set of 600 volumes of the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra was assembled. Of these, 336 volumes designated as cultural properties survive, and 301 volumes have confirmed numbering. Most volumes record this era name in their colophons. During the {{jalink|応永|Q1193078}} era (1394–1428), {{jalink|佐竹貞義|Q11383481|Satake Sadayoshi}} donated copies of the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra bearing his name at the end of the scrolls. In {{jalink|応永|Q1193078}} 21 (1414), {{jalink|佐竹義盛|Q11383456|Satake Yoshimori}} rebuilt the shrine buildings, appointing {{jalink|宍戸氏|Q11451793|Shishido Shirō Yoshitoshi}} and Noda Saburō as construction magistrates, and carved the Satake clan crest, the “Five-ribbed Sun Disc” (five-ribbed folding fan with sun disc), into the ridgepole (ridge tag). Some sources attribute this construction solely to a donation by Shishido Yoshitoshi. In {{jalink|永享|Q1193059}} 12 (1440), during the {{jalink|結城合戦|Q11606564|Yūki War}}, {{jalink|足利持氏|Q4805073|Ashikaga Mochiuji}}’s son {{jalink|足利安王丸|Q21019758|Ashikaga Yasumaru-ō}} offered a vow document and, as thanksgiving, donated one district within the county as shrine rice fields. In {{jalink|永禄|Q1143341}} 12 (1569), {{jalink|佐竹義重 (十八代当主)|Q1133798|Satake Yoshishige}} rebuilt the current main sanctuary (as indicated by the ridge tag). It is also said that {{jalink|佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)|Q1071525|Satake Yoshinobu}}’s coming-of-age ceremony (“Nabiro-me,” {{jalink|元服|Q138321|genpuku}}) was held at this shrine. The area where accompanying retainers rested was called “Satake-nohara,” a place name that survived until the end of the Edo period. === After the Early Modern Period === In {{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}} 7 (1602), the Tokugawa shogunate donated three koku of shrine land, citing established precedent (extant documents). During the {{jalink|正保|Q1146192}} era (1645–1648), {{jalink|笠間城|Q11599983|Kasama Castle}} lord {{jalink|井上正利|Q11373071|Inoue Kawachi-no-kami Masatoshi}} and others dedicated plaques bearing the shrine name. In {{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}} 5 (1677), villagers Adachi Sōzaemon and Adachi Yahyōe of Kamobe Village repaired the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra. When the {{jalink|三河牧野氏|Q2975316|Makino clan}} entered {{jalink|笠間藩|Q3034461|Kasama Domain}}, the shrine became the tutelary shrine of forty-two villages and received ten bundles of rice as ritual offerings. In {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}} 3 (1870), {{jalink|笠間藩|Q3034461|Kasama Domain}} governor {{jalink|牧野貞直|Q10860149|Makino Sadanao}} erected a stone monument inscribed with a poem by {{jalink|葉室光俊|Q23613350|Udaiben Mitsutoshi}}: “**Aoi grass— the many-leaved mountain shrine of Kamo; familiar now, at the season of sacred renewal, how splendid the capital must be.**” In Meiji 6 (1873), under the modern shrine ranking system, the shrine was classified as a village shrine. On June 4, Meiji 12 (1879), it was promoted to the rank of township shrine. On March 7, 1966 (Shōwa 41), 336 volumes of the Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra were designated as Tangible Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture (calligraphic works). In November 1979 (Shōwa 54), Makoto Nakata, mayor of {{jalink|岩瀬町|Q11475420|Iwase Town}}, oversaw repairs to the shrine buildingsFrom the commemorative碑 of shrine rebuilding and repairs within the precincts.. On May 12, 1980 (Shōwa 55), one main sanctuary building of Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property (structure) of Iwase Town. === Shrine Rank === === Divine Rank === **Hitachi Province — Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama-no-Kami** * {{jalink|嘉祥|Q1373157}} 3 (851), 6th month – Listed as an official shrine『{{jalink|日本文徳天皇実録|Q1052905}}』《卷一 嘉祥三年（八五〇）六月己酉【三】》○己酉。雷震西寺刹柱。剥取其竿。中央一許丈。去落於右馬頭藤原朝臣春津宅。詔以武藏國廣瀬神。常陸國鴨大神御子神主玉神。並列於官社。安房國國造正八位上伴直千福麻呂授外從五位下。 **Nushitama-no-Kami** * {{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 3 (862), 9th month – Junior Fifth Rank, Upper『{{jalink|日本三代実録|Q1052981}}』《卷五 貞觀三年（八六一）九月廿三日甲午》○廿三日甲午。授常陸國從五位下主玉神從五位上。 *Gunkyōkō* states that by the Meiō era (1492–1501) the deity had reached Senior Second Rank, and *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* records a subsequent promotion to Senior First Rank. === Modern Period === * {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}} 6 (1873) – Village shrine * June 4, Meiji 12 (1879) – Township shrine === Cultural Properties === === Ibaraki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * {{jalink|大般若波羅蜜多経|Q15905785|Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra}} — 336 volumes (designated March 7, Shōwa 40) === Sakuragawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main sanctuary of Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushitama Shrine === Shrine Precincts === * **Living Torii** ** Instead of a {{jalink|鳥居|Q234731|torii}}, sacred ropes are hung directly on cedar trees. Although torii originally stood here, it is said that after Minamoto no Yoriyoshi displayed the severed head of a defeated mountain hag on the torii as a warning, villagers feared the pollution and her vengeful spirit and dismantled the torii. File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Torii1.JPG|First living torii File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Torii2.JPG|Second living torii File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Torii3.JPG|Third living torii * **Worship Hall, Main Sanctuary** File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Main hall.JPG|Worship hall File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Worship Hall.JPG|Main sanctuary File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Main hall Tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Kamonookaminomikokaminushitama-jinja Kanameishi.JPG|Kaname-ishi (keystone) === Access === * 2 km east of {{jalink|羽黒駅 (茨城県)|Q5638843|Haguro Station}} on the {{jalink|水戸線|Q302388|Mito Line}} of {{jalink|東日本旅客鉄道|Q499071|JR East}}, about a 15-minute walk === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2|refs= Regarding the reading of the shrine name, according to the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Agency registry: *Jinja Kakuroku* reads it as “kamonoōkami mikokaminushitama,” *Jingishiryō* as “kamonoōkami no mikokaminushitama-no,” and *Meiji Jinja Shiryō* (as Kamo Ōkami Mikogami Nushi Shrine) as “kamonoōkami no mikokaminushi-no.” At least in earlier times, “Kamo Ōkami” was read as “kamonoōkami.” According to a shrine history pamphlet distributed by Isobe Inamura Shrine (administrative shrine). A pamphlet post appears among the shrine items on the “[[https://omairi.club/](https://omairi.club/) Omairi]” page for this shrine (viewed April 2020). *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi*, section on shrines. *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* includes a copy of an old document dated Ken’nin 4, 3rd month, 7th day (1204). *Gunkyōkō* gives Ken’nin 3 (1203), stating “a shrine of imperial decree.” *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* includes a copy of an old document dated Teinei 1, 11th month, 21st day (1232). *Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi* includes a copy of an old document dated Genkō 3, 7th month, 26th day (1333). *Hitachi Shiryō Gunkyōkō* (Hitachi Province District and Village Study). The origin legend of Jin’ō-ji states it is a divided spirit of the Raijin Shrine of {{jalink|大阪|Q11439906|Osaka}}. “Osaka” here refers to Ōsaka outside {{jalink|水戸城|Q848997|Mito Castle}} (Kitami-chō, {{jalink|水戸市|Q204249|Mito City}}). According to the shrine tradition of Betsuraikō-ōmikami, the shrine was founded in {{jalink|神亀|Q1373210}} 1 (724), enshrining Betsuraime-no-Mikoto. Originally located in Ōsaka and called Ōsaka Daimyōjin, it was relocated by Satake Yoshinobu to Takajō-machi (around Minami-machi, Mito City) under Jin’ō-ji’s administration, and later relocated again by {{jalink|徳川光圀|Q1194944|Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} (Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album). Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Property Commentary. Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album. *Jōzan Sōsui*. }} === References === * [[https://www.ndl.go.jp/](https://www.ndl.go.jp/) National Diet Library]: Viewable via the [[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/](https://dl.ndl.go.jp/) National Diet Library Digital Collection]. ** Komiyama Masahide, “Suifushiryō, vol. 25.” {{jalink|文化 (元号)|Q1373146|Bunka}} 4 (1807). ** Suzuka Tsuranari et al., “Jinja Kakuroku.” Meiji 3 (1870). ** Nakayama Nobuna, Kurita Hiroshi (eds.), “Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi, lower volume.” Sekizenkansha. Meiji 32–34 (1899–1901). ** Yoshida Tōgo, “Dai Nihon Chimei Jiten, lower volume, 2nd ed.” Fuzanbō. October 17, Meiji 40 (1907). ** Yanagisawa Tsurukichi, “Jōzan Sōsui Meishō Koseki.” Ryūtantō Tokyo Branch. October, Meiji 41 (1908). ** Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office (ed.), “Meiji Jinja Shiryō: Prefectural and Township Shrines (Upper).” Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office. Meiji 45 (1912). ** Ministry of Doctrine (ed.), “Tokusen Shimmeichō.” {{jalink|大正|Q329174|Taishō}} 14 (1925). Isobe Kōyōdō. ** Ibaraki Shimbun, “Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Album.” Ibaraki Shimbunsha. Shōwa 16 (1942). * Miyamoto Mototama, “Hitachi Shiryō Gunkyōkō (Hitachi Province District and Village Study).” Man’en 1 (1860). Available via the [[https://www.nijl.ac.jp/](https://www.nijl.ac.jp/) National Institute of Japanese Literature]. Viewed April 2020. * [[https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/](https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/) Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education], “[[https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/bunkazai/ken/syoseki/5-14/5-14.html](https://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/board/bunkazai/ken/syoseki/5-14/5-14.html) Great Prajñāpāramitā Sutra, 336 volumes].” Cultural Property Commentary. Viewed April 2020. * [[http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/index.html](http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/index.html) Sakuragawa City], “[[http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/page/dir001739.html](http://www.city.sakuragawa.lg.jp/page/dir001739.html) Cultural Properties].” Cultural Property List. Viewed April 2020. {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} * == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:加茂神社]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:茨城県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]
